one_line_takeaway: RSST-NIDS gates temporal-consistency regularization behind a confidence + EMA-teacher-agreement + temporal-stability check, so only traffic windows deemed 'benign-consistent' contribute to the semi-supervised loss, giving 2-3 point F1 gains over SSL baselines in-domain and markedly better cross-dataset AUROC/MCC and poisoning/evasion robustness under a black-box, protocol-constrained adversary.
key_finding: On in-domain CIC-IDS2017 (10% labeled) RSST-NIDS achieves 96.3% accuracy, 94.5% F1, 0.973 AUROC, beating the strongest semi-supervised baseline (CCSSL-style: 95.3%/92.6%/0.965) by roughly 2-3 points absolute F1 and outperforming supervised Transformer (93.1%/90.2%/0.941) and Autoencoder (84.7%/79.3%/0.861). Cross-dataset generalization (train CIC-IDS2017, test zero-shot on CSE-CIC-IDS2018 / UNSW-NB15) shows RSST-NIDS at AUROC 0.889+/-0.008 / 0.847+/-0.010 and MCC 0.61+/-0.02 / 0.57+/-0.03, versus the best baseline CCSSL-style at 0.851/0.814 AUROC and 0.52/0.48 MCC, and supervised Transformer degrading to 0.802/0.765 AUROC and 0.41/0.37 MCC. Per-attack-category F1 shows the largest relative gains on stealthy/low-frequency attacks: Infiltration 62.1 (Transformer) -> 66.8 (SSL-Contrastive) -> 74.3 (RSST-NIDS); Botnet/C2 73.6 -> 77.9 -> 85.4. Under data poisoning of the unlabeled stream, RSST-NIDS AUROC/MCC only mildly degrade as contamination rises (5%: 0.97/0.61 with 42% of unlabeled windows admitted as benign-consistent; 10%: 0.95/0.58, 31% admitted; 15%: 0.93/0.54, 22% admitted), i.e. the gate self-tightens under attack rather than collapsing. Under evasion (traffic morphing, alpha up to 0.8) AUROC drops less than 4%. The false gate rate (malicious windows wrongly admitted as benign-consistent) stays below 6% under nominal conditions. Ablation shows removing confidence-aware pseudo-labeling causes the largest F1 drop (94.5 -> 91.7) and AUROC drop (0.973 -> 0.952), followed by consistency regularization (F1 90.9) and temporal invariance loss (F1 92.1); supervised-only reduces to F1 89.8/AUROC 0.938. Best confidence threshold is tau=0.95, admitting ~35-45% of unlabeled samples, with performance stable within +/-20-40% threshold perturbation. Overhead: 1.3x training time vs. supervised Transformer (2.4h vs 1.8h for 50 epochs), 0.8ms/window inference on GPU (6-8ms CPU), ~1.2GB GPU memory during training.
core_contribution: The paper proposes RSST-NIDS, a robust semi-supervised temporal network intrusion detection framework for cloud environments. Its central novelty is a 'selective temporal invariance' mechanism: instead of enforcing temporal consistency on all unlabeled traffic (as prior semi-supervised NIDS implicitly do by assuming unlabeled data is benign), RSST-NIDS only enforces a latent-space temporal-invariance loss (L_temp) on unlabeled windows that pass a three-part 'benign-consistent' gate: (i) prediction confidence >= tau_b=0.95, (ii) student-teacher (EMA) agreement |f_theta(x)-f_theta'(x)| <= delta_EMA=0.05, and (iii) temporal stability (variance of predictions over a neighborhood of k=2 adjacent windows <= delta_temp=0.01, using a causal/past-only variant at deployment time). This is combined with a Transformer-based temporal encoder (4 layers, 4 heads, hidden size 128), consistency regularization under NetFlow-constraint-preserving augmentations (temporal jitter +/-5%, feature masking 10% on non-causal features only, Gaussian noise sigma=0.01), and confidence-aware pseudo-labeling, in a joint loss L_total = L_sup + lambda_c*L_cons + lambda_p*L_pseudo + lambda_t*L_temp (lambda_c=1.0, lambda_p=0.5, lambda_t=0.2). The EMA teacher (decay alpha=0.99) is explicitly NOT used to generate pseudo-labels or global consistency targets -- it serves only as a conservative reference signal for the benign-consistency gate, which the authors distinguish from prior work like Mean Teacher.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with no access to model parameters or gradients, operating under protocol/feature validity constraints (NetFlow semantics: non-negativity, bounded rates, packet/byte consistency). Two capabilities are modeled: (1) Poisoning -- injecting malicious flows into the unlabeled training stream at contamination rates of 5-15%, crafted to match benign traffic in first-order statistics (duration, packet count, byte rate) while preserving attack semantics and temporal structure; (2) Evasion -- test-time traffic morphing via convex blending x_adv = alpha*x_attack + (1-alpha)*x_benign with alpha in [0.6, 0.8], projected onto the valid NetFlow feature space, including transfer-based (surrogate-model-tuned) black-box attacks. White-box attacks (gradient access) are explicitly stated as out of scope, described by the authors as 'less realistic at the network level.' The paper explicitly disclaims worst-case robustness, framing the goal as raising the cost of evasion rather than guaranteeing resistance to an adaptive worst-case adversary.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) white-box adversaries (gradient/parameter access) are out of scope and the method does not claim worst-case robustness, only raising the cost of evasion; (2) the current design is binary (benign vs. malicious) only -- extending to multi-class attribution is left as future work, and the authors flag that under severe class imbalance, pseudo-label confidence becomes less reliable for minority attack classes and benign-consistency gating may bias learning toward dominant classes; (3) no explicit adversarial-training or certified-robustness defenses are integrated (left to future work); (4) no host-level context / service-dependency graph is used, limiting detection of multi-stage or lateral-movement attacks; (5) no online/continual learning mechanism for long-term traffic evolution is evaluated; (6) no privacy-preserving or federated learning setting is evaluated despite motivating privacy as a design goal for using only flow-level (non-payload) features; (7) interpretability (post-hoc explanations, feature attribution) is not addressed. All evaluation is offline on three public, English-language, mostly enterprise-style benchmark datasets (CIC-IDS2017, CSE-CIC-IDS2018, UNSW-NB15) -- no live cloud deployment or production traffic validation is reported.

datasets_used: CIC-IDS2017 (primary train/val/test; 180K/40K/60K windows; 18.5%/17.9%/18.2% attack ratio; day-based split: Mon/Tue train, Wed val, Thu/Fri test)
CSE-CIC-IDS2018 (cross-dataset zero-shot test only; 120K windows; 16.7% attack ratio)
UNSW-NB15 (cross-dataset zero-shot test only; 100K windows; 14.2% attack ratio)
quantitative_results: In-domain CIC-IDS2017 (10% labeled): RSST-NIDS 96.3% accuracy / 94.5% F1 / 0.973 AUROC
Best SSL baseline (CCSSL-style) in-domain: 95.3% accuracy / 92.6% F1 / 0.965 AUROC
Supervised Transformer in-domain: 93.1% accuracy / 90.2% F1 / 0.941 AUROC
Cross-dataset CIC->CSE-CIC: RSST-NIDS AUROC 0.889+/-0.008, MCC 0.61+/-0.02 vs. CCSSL-style AUROC 0.851+/-0.010, MCC 0.52+/-0.03
Cross-dataset CIC->UNSW-NB15: RSST-NIDS AUROC 0.847+/-0.010, MCC 0.57+/-0.03 vs. supervised Transformer AUROC 0.765+/-0.014, MCC 0.37+/-0.04
Per-attack F1 (Transformer / SSL-Contrastive / RSST-NIDS): Infiltration 62.1/66.8/74.3; Botnet-C2 73.6/77.9/85.4; DoS-DDoS 88.3/90.7/94.9
Poisoning robustness: 5% contamination -> AUROC 0.97, MCC 0.61, 42% unlabeled windows admitted; 10% -> 0.95/0.58/31%; 15% -> 0.93/0.54/22%
Evasion robustness: AUROC drops <4% at morphing parameter alpha=0.8; false gate rate <6% under nominal conditions

Ablation on CIC-IDS2017 F1/AUROC: Full model 94.5/0.973; minus confidence-aware pseudo-labeling 91.7/0.952; minus temporal invariance loss 92.1/0.957; minus consistency regularization 90.9/0.946; supervised-only 89.8/0.938
Best pseudo-label threshold tau=0.95, admitting ~35-45% of unlabeled samples
Compute overhead: 1.3x training time vs supervised Transformer (2.4h vs 1.8h/50 epochs); inference 0.8ms/window (GPU), 6-8ms (CPU); ~1.2GB GPU training memory
baselines_compared: CCSSL / class-aware SSL with confidence-weighted pseudo-labels and contrastive learning (semi-supervised)
Mean Teacher (semi-supervised, perturbation consistency without temporal gating)

Excluded from empirical comparison (differing assumptions, discussed only in Related Work): AnomalyAID (reconstruction-based pseudo-label verification), SS-VTCN (semi-supervised variational temporal convolutional network), DI-NIDS (domain-invariant NIDS requiring target-domain access)
